figures.caption: Figure 4 | The KSA cache features organized layout of contiguous tensors to enable efficient memory access. The lifecycle involves writing new tokens, inserting summaries, and recycling chunks in place.
Figure 6 | Overall Distribution of Pretraining Data Proportions.

Table 4 | For CPT setting, hybrid-KSA achieves superior long-context retrieval across all RULER lengths, while both KSA variants closely match or exceed Full attention on knowledge, math, and coding benchmarks, establishing the smallest performance gap among all sub-quadratic alternatives.
Figure 11 | Per-layer attention patterns reveal thatHybrid-KSA enhances cross-chunk retrieval through “comb-like” summary attention and full-layer integration.
Table 2 | Training model configurations of our KSA.

Figure 2 | Our proposed Kwai Summary Attention (KSA), maintains sub-quadratic complexity while preserving the expressivity in modeling long-range dependencies. By interleaving KSA with full attention blocks, hybrid-KSA achieves satisfactory balance between performance and efficiency.
Figure 8 | Hybrid-KSA delivers the best training dynamics, with superior convergence efficiency and faster performance gains across benchmarks in from-scratch training.
Table 6 | For CPT, Hybrid-KSA achieves the best overall results on RULER 128K subtasks, beating all other efficient baselines and Full attention on NIAH-Multivalue, VT, FWE, and SQuAD.

Figure 9 | NIAH-Single results after converting the model into a hybrid architecture and applying continual pre-training. Hybrid-KSA achieves near-perfect needle retrieval up to 128K context length, with only a minor drop from full accuracy at 128K.

Figure 7 | The training recipes for CPT warmup: (a) insert additional Q/K/V learnable weights to adapt the summary token compression; (b) conduct the annealing strategy to drop the summary weights; (c) full parameter tuning on large data corpus. Residual connections are omitted for brevity.
Figure 5 | KV cache size comparison across different mechanisms as sequence length grows.
Figure 10 | Hybrid-KSA reduces KV cache by up to 2.5× while maintaining competitive or higher decode throughput compared with other efficient baselines.

Figure 3 | Sliding Window Attention (left) may cut through a chunk, resulting attention to overlook certain tokens at boundaries and causing information loss (red regions). Sliding Chunk Attention (right) aligns window boundaries with chunk boundaries, ensuring clean information routing.
Table 5 | Hybrid-KSA consistently outperforms full and sub-quadratic baselines, achieving superior long-context scalability and strong general performance in from-scratch training.
